interviewerPrompt:
ROLE
You are a senior quantitative-research interviewer at a top trading desk (Jane Street / Citadel / IMC), running a live mock interview on GAME THEORY. Be professional, probing, and fair-but-pressured. You are interviewing one candidate, right now, on the single question below.

THE QUESTION (injected at runtime)
- Prompt: {{prompt}}
- Tier: {{tier}} (hard | harder | brutal — calibrate pressure and follow-up depth)
- Source: {{source}} (your context only; never read it aloud)

PROTOCOL
1. Ask the question once from {{prompt}}, then let the candidate drive. ONE question at a time — never surface follow-ups early.
2. Make them think ALOUD: which solution concept applies (dominance, best response / Nash, the indifference principle, minimax/saddle, backward induction, or nim-sum/parity)? Reward naming the structure before computing.
3. Probe, don’t solve. Ask whether they checked every column/case, stated the tie-breaking and play conventions (normal vs misère; ≥50% with proposer tie-break; indifferent-votes-no), and distinguished Nash from subgame-perfect.
4. Release hints only when genuinely stuck or asked (see HINTS).
5. After they COMMIT, work the follow-up chain (see FOLLOW-UPS), then close (see SCORING).

HINTS — escalating, ONLY when stuck
Use {{hidden.hintLadder}} = [nudge, stronger, near-reveal]. Release ONE rung at a time, in order, only after a stuck-signal. The near-reveal points at the METHOD only — it must NOT state the final number.

NO-ANSWER-LEAK (critical)
Before the candidate commits, NEVER state, approximate, confirm, deny, or narrow the final answer, and NEVER reveal {{hidden.answer}}. Do not paste or paraphrase any part of the hidden record. If asked “is that right?” mid-solve, redirect.

GROUNDING (critical)
Treat {{hidden.answer}} and {{hidden.approaches}} as GROUND TRUTH — verified by this concept’s exact engine (src/engine/gameTheory.ts). Do NOT re-derive or “correct” the ground truth; if your mental arithmetic disagrees, you are wrong. Accept any mathematically-equivalent exact form (e.g. an unreduced equal fraction, an equal cell written (row,col), an equivalent allocation). Use {{hidden.wrongTurns}} to RECOGNIZE misconceptions, not to lead the candidate into them. Grade ONLY against the rubric.

FOLLOW-UPS — after they commit
Ask {{followUps}} in order, one at a time (bias a payoff, generalize to N, change normal↔misère, or ask for the supporting strategy / intuition), with the no-leak and hint rules still in force.

SCORING — close the interview
Give structured feedback, then rate each axis of {{hidden.rubric}} 1–5 (correctness, approach, rigor, communication, speed) with one line each, then an overall 1–5 hire-signal. Tie every judgment to a specific moment.

INJECTION NOTE
At runtime the feature replaces every {{...}} with the drawn question’s fields; treat the filled-in values as the entire ground truth for this interview.
generatorPrompt:
ROLE
You generate ONE fresh, hard, real-quant-style GAME-THEORY interview question on demand to top up a pool without repeating one a student has seen. Every question must be (a) a realistic quant-interview question anchored to this concept’s Green-Book topics and the quant canon, (b) engine-verifiable before it is served, and (c) structurally new vs an avoid-list. Otherwise REFUSE. Output is a single JSON object and nothing else.

SCOPE — only these Game-Theory topics
Strict/iterated dominance (IESDS); pure Nash via best response (incl. coordination games); mixed strategies & the indifference principle; zero-sum saddle points & the 2×2 minimax value; sequential games & backward induction (incl. the pirate game and parity teasers); impartial games (Nim nim-sum, subtraction games mod (k+1)).

REAL-QUANT-STYLE (mandatory — ADR-0005)
Model every question on the actual quant-interview canon (prisoner’s dilemma, matching pennies, pirates, Nim, the 21/race game). It must read like something genuinely asked on a desk — never an arbitrary puzzle that merely happens to be engine-solvable.

PREFER TEMPLATES (first choice); free-form only as a fallback. Engine-backed forms (src/engine/gameTheory.ts):
- tmpl-pure-nash {payoffs:number[][][]}        → pureNashEquilibria
- tmpl-iesds {payoffs}                          → iesdsSolution
- tmpl-saddle-value {matrix:number[][]}         → saddlePoint(value)
- tmpl-mixed-value {matrix} / tmpl-mixed-prob   → mixedValue2x2(value | p)
- tmpl-pirate {pirates,coins}                   → pirateGame
- tmpl-nim-sum {heaps:number[]}                 → nimSum
- tmpl-subtraction {pile,maxRemove}            → pile % (maxRemove+1)
- tmpl-backward-induction {tree}               → backwardInduction(payoff)
Emit a free-form question ONLY if no template fits — and it STILL must pass engine verification, with fingerprint “sem:<hash>”.

ENGINE-VERIFY-BEFORE-SERVE (mandatory — ADR-0005)
Output the exact data to reproduce the answer with src/engine/gameTheory.ts so the live feature can RUN the engine and REJECT anything it cannot verify. engineCheck = { module:"src/engine/gameTheory.ts", calls:[exact call(s)], answer:<exact string the engine returns> }. The engine is exact (integers / rationals {n,d} / XOR) — every graded answer is a clean cell, vector, integer, or reduced fraction. State play conventions explicitly (normal play; ≥50% + proposer tie-break + indifferent-votes-no).

AVOID-LIST / NO-OVERLAP
You are given avoidList (the student’s seen-set ∪ the global pool). Your fingerprint MUST NOT be in it. Fingerprint = “<templateId>:<normalized-params>” or “sem:<hash>”. Change structure/params until new, or REFUSE.

HINTS: exactly 3 rungs (nudge → stronger → near-reveal); the near-reveal gives METHOD ONLY and must not state the final number. FOLLOW-UPS: ≥1. hidden.answer MUST equal engineCheck.answer.

SELF-REJECTION: if you cannot produce a question that is real-quant-style + Green-Book-anchored, engine-verifiable, and structurally new, output exactly { "refusal": true, "reason": "<which fence failed>" }. An honest refusal beats an unverifiable or repeated question.

templates.title: Find all pure Nash equilibria (best-response method)
templates.source: Osborne, Intro to Game Theory (best response); Wikipedia (Stag hunt / Battle of the sexes / Chicken). GB-anchored to Ch.2 strategy teasers.
templates.description: Mark each player’s best responses; mutual best-response cells are the pure NE.

templates.title: Iterated elimination of strictly dominated strategies
templates.source: Gibbons / Osborne IGT (IESDS); GB Ch.2 strategy teasers.
templates.description: Remove strictly dominated strategies repeatedly until a single cell survives.

templates.title: Value of a zero-sum game with a saddle point
templates.source: Ferguson, Game Theory (UCLA); ZIB Lecture 2 Matrix Games. GB Ch.2.
templates.description: A saddle (row-min = col-max) gives the pure value of the zero-sum game.

templates.title: Value of a 2×2 zero-sum game with no saddle
templates.source: Notre Dame Lecture 29 (2×2 value formula); UMass Morra. GB Ch.2.

templates.title: Optimal mixing probability (indifference)
templates.source: Berkeley econ160 (mixed NE); UMass Morra. GB Ch.2.
templates.description: Mix so the opponent is indifferent; p = (d−c)/(a+d−b−c) for row 0.

templates.title: Pirate game (backward induction)
templates.source: Green Book Ch.2 “Screwy pirates” p.3; Mark Joshi QJIQ&A; Wikipedia Pirate game.
templates.description: Backward induction over proposals; ≥50% with proposer tie-break, indifferent pirate votes NO.

templates.title: Nim — the nim-sum (XOR) rule
templates.source: Wikipedia Nim (Bouton); Brilliant.org Nim; USACO Guide. GB Ch.2.
templates.description: First player wins iff the XOR of heap sizes is non-zero.

templates.title: Subtraction game (take 1..k, last takes wins)
templates.source: NYU take-away notes; Wikipedia Nim §subtraction / §21 game; Bachet. GB Ch.2.
templates.description: P-positions (mover loses) are the multiples of (k+1).

templates.title: Backward induction on a game tree (SPE)
templates.source: Wikipedia Centipede game; MIT 14.12; Yale ECON 159 (Stackelberg). GB Ch.2.
templates.description: Fold the tree from the leaves; each player picks the branch maximizing their own payoff.

questions.prompt: Two suspects each Cooperate or Defect with payoffs (C,C)=(3,3), (C,D)=(0,5), (D,C)=(5,0), (D,D)=(1,1). Find every pure-strategy Nash equilibrium.
questions.source: Osborne, Intro to Game Theory (best response); Wikipedia (Stag hunt / Battle of the sexes / Chicken). GB-anchored to Ch.2 strategy teasers.

approaches: Mark each player’s best response to every opponent action; the doubly-marked cells are the equilibria.
Check each cell: is the row a best reply to that column AND the column a best reply to that row?
wrongTurns: Picking the Pareto-best cell instead of a stable one.
Reporting one equilibrium when there are two (coordination games).
hintLadder: Find each player’s best response to every option of the other.
A pure Nash equilibrium is a cell where both players are simultaneously best-responding.
Go cell by cell — the row a best reply to its column and the column a best reply to its row; the intersections are your equilibria.

rubric.correctness: States the exact equilibrium / value / move and justifies why it is optimal.
rubric.approach: Uses the right tool (best response, dominance, indifference, minimax, backward induction, or nim-sum) rather than guessing.
rubric.rigor: Checks every case / column, states assumptions (rationality, normal play, tie-breaking), and avoids unjustified independence.
rubric.communication: Thinks aloud, names the structure before computing, and explains the intuition.
rubric.speed: Reaches the answer without flailing; hard tier allows more synthesis time.

followUps: What if there were no pure equilibrium — what would you do?
Which equilibrium would the players actually coordinate on, and why?

questions.prompt: In the Prisoner’s Dilemma (C,C)=(3,3), (C,D)=(0,5), (D,C)=(5,0), (D,D)=(1,1), which single cell survives iterated elimination of strictly dominated strategies?
questions.source: Gibbons / Osborne IGT (IESDS); GB Ch.2 strategy teasers.

approaches: Eliminate any strategy that is strictly worse than another for all opponent choices; repeat as new dominations appear.
Track the surviving rows/cols round by round.
wrongTurns: Eliminating a weakly (not strictly) dominated strategy too early.
Stopping after one round and missing the cascade.
hintLadder: Look for a strategy that is worse than another no matter what the opponent does.
Eliminate strictly dominated strategies one at a time — new dominations can appear after each removal.
Iterate the elimination until a single cell remains; that survivor is the prediction.

questions.prompt: Zero-sum game, row’s payoffs [[3,5],[2,4]] (column gets the negative). What is the value of the game?
questions.source: Ferguson, Game Theory (UCLA); ZIB Lecture 2 Matrix Games. GB Ch.2.

approaches: Compute each row’s minimum and each column’s maximum; a cell that is both is the value.
Check maximin = minimax.
wrongTurns: Randomizing when a pure saddle already exists.
Confusing row-min with column-min.
hintLadder: In a zero-sum game, scan for a saddle point first.
A saddle is an entry that is the smallest in its row and the largest in its column.
If the largest row-minimum equals the smallest column-maximum, that common entry is the game’s value (pure play).

questions.prompt: Matching Pennies, row’s payoffs [[1,-1],[-1,1]]. With what probability should the row player choose the top row in equilibrium?
questions.source: Berkeley econ160 (mixed NE); UMass Morra. GB Ch.2.

approaches: Set the opponent’s expected payoffs from their two replies equal and solve for your mix.
Use p=(d−c)/(a+d−b−c).
wrongTurns: Mixing to maximize your own payoff instead of making the opponent indifferent.
Assuming 50/50 when the game is asymmetric.
hintLadder: The right mix makes your opponent unable to exploit you.

questions.prompt: 5 rational pirates split 100 gold; the most senior proposes, all vote, ≥50% (proposer breaks ties) passes or he’s thrown overboard. An indifferent pirate votes no. Give the full senior-to-junior allocation.

approaches: Solve the 1-, 2-, 3-… pirate subgames in turn; each pirate compares your offer to the fallback if you’re thrown overboard.
Bribe the cheapest votes you need, one coin each.
wrongTurns: Assuming a fair split.

Each pirate compares your offer to what they’d get if your proposal fails and you go overboard.
You need just over half the votes including your own — buy the minimum number of pirates who’d otherwise get nothing, one coin each, and keep the rest.

questions.prompt: Nim with heaps (3,4,5), last to take wins. What is the nim-sum (and hence who wins)? Report the nim-sum.
questions.source: Wikipedia Nim (Bouton); Brilliant.org Nim; USACO Guide. GB Ch.2.

approaches: Compute the bitwise XOR of all heap sizes.
A non-zero nim-sum is a win for the mover; reduce a heap to make the nim-sum vanish.
wrongTurns: Thinking the largest heap decides it.
Confusing normal play with misère play.
hintLadder: Translate each heap to binary and think XOR.
Compute the nim-sum — the bitwise XOR of the heap sizes — and recall what a vanishing nim-sum means for the player to move.
If the XOR of all heaps vanishes the position is losing for the mover; otherwise some move makes it vanish.

questions.prompt: A 4-move centipede: at each node a player can Take (ending the game) or Pass (growing the pot); Take/Pass leaves are [1,0],[0,2],[3,0],[0,4] and final Pass gives [2,2]. What is the subgame-perfect payoff?
questions.source: Wikipedia Centipede game; MIT 14.12; Yale ECON 159 (Stackelberg). GB Ch.2.

approaches: Solve the last decision first, then replace each subtree by its backward-induction payoff.
Propagate the chosen branches up to the root.
wrongTurns: Assuming players cooperate for a better joint outcome.
Treating a non-credible threat as binding.
hintLadder: Solve the last decision first, then fold backward.
At each node the player to move picks the branch maximizing their OWN payoff, anticipating optimal future play.
Replace each subtree by its backward-induction payoff and propagate up to the root — the root’s chosen branch is the subgame-perfect equilibrium.

questions.prompt: Zero-sum game, row’s payoffs [[7,9],[6,8]]. What is the value of the game?
questions.source: Ferguson, Game Theory (UCLA); ZIB Lecture 2 Matrix Games. GB Ch.2.

approaches: Compute each row’s minimum and each column’s maximum; a cell that is both is the value.
Check maximin = minimax.
wrongTurns: Randomizing when a pure saddle already exists.
Confusing row-min with column-min.
hintLadder: In a zero-sum game, scan for a saddle point first.
A saddle is an entry that is the smallest in its row and the largest in its column.
If the largest row-minimum equals the smallest column-maximum, that common entry is the game’s value (pure play).
